title: Per-token approvals are not cleared on owner or operator transfers, allowing stale approved addresses to steal NFTs
claim: `approval[tokenId]` is only cleared in the branch where the approved address itself calls `transferFrom`/`safeTransferFrom`. When the owner or an `approvalAll` operator moves the NFT, `_transfer` updates balances and ownership but never resets the old single-token approval. That stale approved address remains authorized against the new owner and can later call `transferFrom` to seize the token. The same stale approval also survives a burn because `burn()` calls `_transfer` directly.
impact: A previously approved address can steal NFTs after they are sold or otherwise transferred by the owner/operator. It can also reclaim a burned NFT from `DEAD_ADDRESS` without paying the 5 DAO restore cost, bypassing the intended restoration payment flow.
paths: Owner approves attacker for token `id` via `approve(attacker, id)` -> owner or operator transfers token to a buyer via `transferFrom` or `safeTransferFrom` -> approval is not cleared -> attacker calls `transferFrom(buyer, attacker, id)` and succeeds.

Owner approves attacker for token `id` -> owner calls `burn(id)` -> token moves to `DEAD_ADDRESS` but approval persists -> attacker calls `transferFrom(DEAD_ADDRESS, attacker, id)` and restores the NFT without paying DAO fees.

title: ERC165 advertises enumerable and receiver interfaces that the contract does not actually honor
claim: `supportsInterface()` returns true for `IERC721Enumerable` and `IERC721TokenReceiver`, but the implementation does not satisfy either interface's expected behavior. `totalSupply()` returns `max` rather than the number of assigned tokens, `tokenOfOwnerByIndex()` ignores `_owner` and simply returns `_index` if any token is assigned there, and `onERC721Received()` always reverts instead of accepting transfers and returning the selector.
impact: Integrators that trust ERC165 can mis-enumerate ownership, miscount supply, or attempt safe transfers into this contract and hit unexpected reverts. That can break marketplace indexing, custody flows, and other integrations built around standard ERC721 behavior.
paths: Integrator checks `supportsInterface(type(IERC721Enumerable).interfaceId)` -> uses `totalSupply`/`tokenOfOwnerByIndex` to enumerate holdings -> receives token IDs unrelated to the queried owner or unminted IDs -> downstream accounting or UI logic misbehaves.

External contract or bridge checks `supportsInterface(type(IERC721TokenReceiver).interfaceId)` -> attempts `safeTransferFrom` into this contract -> `onERC721Received()` reverts and the transfer path fails unexpectedly.

title: Approval events log the caller instead of the actual token owner
claim: The contract emits `Approval(msg.sender, ...)` instead of `Approval(owner, ...)`. When an operator approves a spender, or when an approved spender clears approval during transfer, the log's indexed owner field does not match the token's true owner.
impact: Off-chain systems that reconstruct approval state from logs can desynchronize from on-chain reality, causing incorrect approval displays, stale listings, or misattributed authorization history.
paths: Owner grants `setApprovalForAll(operator, true)` -> operator calls `approve(spender, tokenId)` -> emitted `Approval` event records `operator` as owner even though `ownerOf(tokenId)` is unchanged.
Approved spender transfers the token -> contract emits approval-clear event with the spender in the owner field -> log-based indexers can mis-handle approval invalidation.
